text:
*[Bug 18743] "hclust()" documentation about "ward.D" and "ward.D2" need to be revised. In addition to the incorrect description of the "ward.D" and "ward.D2" options, there is a lack of correct information about the value of d to be set.*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18743>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
         Resolution|---                         |FIXED
             Status|ASSIGNED                    |CLOSED

--- Comment #12 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
I have committed another sentence to the help file and updated / added new
examples and comments to them to R-devel in svn rev 90071 

```
hcity <- list(
    D.o = hclust(UScitiesD, "ward.D"), # "wrong"
    D2o = hclust(UScitiesD, "ward.D2"),# similar to next two
    D   = hclust(1/2* (UScitiesD)^2,  "ward.D"),  # Ward(1963) ESS  based
    D2  = hclust(sqrt(1/2)*UScitiesD, "ward.D2")) #       sqrt(ESS) based

opar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mgp = c(1.2, .3, 0), mar = .1 + c(3,4,2,1))
 plot(hcity$D.o, hang=-1, col=2) # different clustering than the others
 plot(hcity$D2o, hang=-1) # [1,2]
 plot(hcity$D,   hang=-1) # [2,1]
 plot(hcity$D2,  hang=-1) # [2,2] as above, [1,2], just y-axis numbers differ
par(opar)
mrg <- sapply(hcity, `[[`, "merge") # defining cluster tree (apart from height)
ht  <- sapply(hcity, `[[`, "height")
stopifnot(exprs = {
   any(mrg[,2] != mrg[,1])
       mrg[,2] == mrg[,3:4] # all eq., but the first
   all.equal(ht[,"D2" ], sqrt(ht[,"D"]))
   all.equal(ht[,"D2"], sqrt(1/2)*ht[,"D2o"])
})
```

In my view this now explains the relationship between `"ward.D"` and
`"ward.D2"`;
it *is* still true that using `"ward.D"` with*out* explicitly squaring the
distances is _"wrong"_ .

I'm closing this bug report now. Thank you, Atsushi Kimura, notably for
providing an English translation to your article.

text:
*[Bug 18364] Initializing Column Improper Length*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18364>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
             Status|CLOSED                      |REOPENED
         Resolution|FIXED                       |---

--- Comment #13 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
(In reply to Gabriel Becker from comment #12)
> Created attachment 3710
> Revision of doc patch
> 
> Explicitly differentiates `x$c[3:4]<-5` and `x[3:4, "c"] <-5` behavior as
> pointed bout by Suharto

Thank you, Gabe.
I have also worked to add an example of this behaviour to the help page,
and found the following ... which I *would* count as a bug .. (!)

```
> sw <- swiss[1:5, 1:4]
> sw$c5[c(1,5)] <- c("J","K"); sw
             Fertility Agriculture Examination Education   c5
Courtelary        80.2        17.0          15        12    J
Delemont          83.1        45.1           6         9 <NA>
Franches-Mnt      92.5        39.7           5         5 <NA>
Moutier           85.8        36.5          12         7 <NA>
Neuveville        76.9        43.5          17        15    K
> sw$c6[1:2] <- c("J","K") 
Error in `$<-.data.frame`(`*tmp*`, c6, value = c("J", "K")) : 
  replacement has 2 rows, data has 5
> 
```
in the working case, we do explicitly assign to the last row (`5`), in the
error case, we don't, but I think according to all we said above (and on the
help page), the two subassignments should either both work (filling in NAs), or
fail.
I'd currently suggest that the 2nd case should work, too, giving a 
(J K NA NA NA) column.

--- Comment #14 from Suharto Anggono (<mailto:suharto_anggono@yahoo.com|suharto_anggono@yahoo.com>) ---
(In reply to Martin Maechler from comment #13)
> (In reply to Gabriel Becker from comment #12)
> > Created attachment 3710
> > Revision of doc patch
> > 
> > Explicitly differentiates `x$c[3:4]<-5` and `x[3:4, "c"] <-5` behavior as

> > sw$c5[c(1,5)] <- c("J","K"); sw

> Courtelary        80.2        17.0          15        12    J
> Delemont          83.1        45.1           6         9 <NA>
> Franches-Mnt      92.5        39.7           5         5 <NA>
> Moutier           85.8        36.5          12         7 <NA>
> Neuveville        76.9        43.5          17        15    K
> > sw$c6[1:2] <- c("J","K") 
> Error in `$<-.data.frame`(`*tmp*`, c6, value = c("J", "K")) : 
>   replacement has 2 rows, data has 5
> > 
> ```
> in the working case, we do explicitly assign to the last row (`5`), in the
> error case, we don't, but I think according to all we said above (and on the
> help page), the two subassignments should either both work (filling in NAs),
> or fail.
> I'd currently suggest that the 2nd case should work, too, giving a 
> (J K NA NA NA) column.

But what is said above is not true.

Please see the example in Comment 10 and its result in Comment 11.

Martin Maechler also has answered the Stack Overflow question
<https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72618429/why-does-r-allow-new-column-creation-via-tail-slicing/>
. The answer mentions recycling.

What is done by `$` replacement method is documented:

> The replacement method (for `$`) checks
> `value` for the correct number of rows, and replicates it if necessary.

text:
*[Bug 19015] R segmentation fault in Windows with empty -e argument*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19015>

Trevor Davis (<mailto:trevor.l.davis@gmail.com|trevor.l.davis@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |trevor.l.davis@gmail.com

--- Comment #2 from Trevor Davis (<mailto:trevor.l.davis@gmail.com|trevor.l.davis@gmail.com>) ---
Created attachment 3712
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3712&action=edit>
Minimal patch just to `R_Suicide` to guard against an unset `ptr_Suicide`

> On Windows, passing an empty string to Rscript -e "" causes a crash

I observe that this crash still occurs in R-devel for Windows

Note: If you look at `src/gnuwin32/system.c` there are actually several
locations in the code where `R_Suicide()` could null-dereference an unset
`ptr_Suicide` and cause a crash:

Inside `R_SetWin32()` before line 961 when `ptr_Suicide` is first set:

- 906: embedded use without R_HOME set
- 909: malloc failure for R_Home
- 925: malloc(30) failure for gccversion

In `cmdlineoptions()` (before `R_SetParams()` is called at line 1361 which
calls `R_SetWin32()`):

- 1123: getRHOME() returned NULL
- 1269, 1279, 1291, 1302: various malformed option arguments 
- 1325: -e combined with -f/--file
- 1349: tmpfile creation failure (bad TMPDIR)
- 1353: fwrite error writing the -e expression to tmpfile

Bill Denney's patch also added null-guards for five more functions---two for
Windows and three for Unix (but seemingly only used for Solaris)---but to fix
just this exact family of crashes in Windows you actually only need a minimal
patch to `R_Suicide` to guard against an unset `ptr_Suicide`.  Alternatively
you could probably make sure to always set `ptr_Suicide` before using it.
